annotations.label: Travelling salesman problem (TSP)

anchor.snippet: the travelling salesman problem ( TSP ) asks the following question

annotations.note: No definition of the travelling salesman problem appears anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Decision version of TSP

anchor.snippet: The decision version of the TSP (where given a length L , the task is to decide whether the graph has a tour whose length is at most L )

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of the TSP decision problem.

annotations.label: Decision TSP is NP-complete

anchor.snippet: belongs to the class of NP-complete problems

annotations.note: Mathlib contains no complexity-theoretic notions such as NP-completeness.

annotations.label: Beardwood–Halton–Hammersley theorem (intro)

anchor.snippet: The Beardwood–Halton–Hammersley theorem provides a practical solution to the travelling salesman problem

annotations.note: No mention of Beardwood, Halton, or Hammersley anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Christofides–Serdyukov 1.5-approximation

anchor.snippet: the Christofides–Serdyukov algorithm yields a solution that, in the worst case, is at most 1.5 times longer than the optimal solution

annotations.note: No mention of Christofides or Serdyukov anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Karp: Hamiltonian cycle is NP-complete implies TSP NP-hard

anchor.snippet: Richard M. Karp showed in 1972 that the Hamiltonian cycle problem was NP-complete , which implies the NP-hardness of TSP

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `SimpleGraph.IsHamiltonian` but has no complexity classes (NP, NP-hard).

annotations.label: TSP as a graph problem

anchor.snippet: TSP can be modeled as an undirected weighted graph

annotations.note: Mathlib has `SimpleGraph` but no weighted-graph TSP model.

anchor.snippet: In the symmetric TSP , the distance between two cities is the same in each opposite direction
In the asymmetric TSP , paths may not exist in both directions or the distances might be different

annotations.label: TSP as Hamiltonian cycle problem

anchor.snippet: An equivalent formulation in terms of graph theory is

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Hamiltonian cycles via `SimpleGraph.Walk.IsHamiltonianCycle`, but not the TSP equivalence.

annotations.label: Bottleneck travelling salesman problem

anchor.snippet: Another related problem is the bottleneck travelling salesman problem

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define the bottleneck TSP.

annotations.label: Generalized travelling salesman problem

anchor.snippet: The generalized travelling salesman problem , also known as the "travelling politician problem"

annotations.note: No generalized TSP definition exists in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: Noon and Bean demonstrated that the generalized travelling salesman problem can be transformed into a standard TSP

annotations.label: Sequential ordering problem

anchor.snippet: The sequential ordering problem deals with the problem of visiting a set of cities, where precedence relations between the cities exist

annotations.note: No sequential ordering problem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Travelling purchaser problem

anchor.snippet: The travelling purchaser problem deals with a purchaser who is charged with purchasing a set of products

annotations.note: No travelling purchaser problem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: TSP as an integer linear program

anchor.snippet: The TSP can be formulated as an integer linear program

annotations.note: Mathlib has no integer linear programming infrastructure for TSP.

anchor.snippet: The MTZ formulation of TSP is thus the following integer linear programming problem
Then TSP can be written as the following integer linear programming problem

annotations.label: Subtour elimination constraint

anchor.snippet: The last constraint of the DFJ formulation—called a subtour elimination constraint

annotations.note: No subtour elimination constraint in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Brute-force permutation search

anchor.snippet: The most direct solution would be to try all permutations

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize a brute-force TSP search.

anchor.snippet: One of the earliest applications of dynamic programming is the Held–Karp algorithm

annotations.label: Ambainis et al. quantum exact algorithm

anchor.snippet: The currently best quantum exact algorithm for TSP due to Ambainis et al.

annotations.note: No quantum TSP algorithm in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nearest neighbour algorithm

anchor.snippet: The nearest neighbour (NN) algorithm (a greedy algorithm ) lets the salesman choose the nearest unvisited city as his next move

annotations.note: No nearest-neighbour TSP heuristic in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rosenkrantz NN approximation factor

anchor.snippet: showed that the NN algorithm has the approximation factor

annotations.note: No Rosenkrantz approximation bound in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bitonic tour

anchor.snippet: The bitonic tour of a set of points is the minimum-perimeter monotone polygon that has the points as its vertices

annotations.note: No bitonic tour definition in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: Another constructive heuristic , Match Twice and Stitch (MTS), performs two sequential matchings

anchor.section: The Algorithm of Christofides and Serdyukov
anchor.snippet: The algorithm of Christofides and Serdyukov follows a similar outline

anchor.section: The Algorithm of Christofides and Serdyukov
anchor.snippet: This gives a TSP tour which is at most 1.5 times the optimal

annotations.label: Eulerian tour lower bound via triangle inequality

anchor.section: The Algorithm of Christofides and Serdyukov
anchor.snippet: By the triangle inequality , we know that the TSP tour can be no longer than the Eulerian tour

annotations.note: Mathlib mentions Eulerian trails in `SimpleGraph.Trails` but no TSP-Eulerian bound.

anchor.snippet: The pairwise exchange or 2-opt technique involves iteratively removing two edges and replacing them
the 3-opt technique removes 3 edges and reconnects them to form a shorter tour
The Lin–Kernighan heuristic is a special case of the V -opt or variable-opt technique
The variable-opt method is related to, and a generalization of, the k -opt method

annotations.label: Constricting Insertion Heuristic

anchor.snippet: This starts with a sub-tour such as the convex hull and then inserts other vertices

annotations.note: No constricting insertion heuristic in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ant colony system (ACS)

anchor.snippet: Marco Dorigo described in 1993 a method of heuristically generating "good solutions" to the TSP using a simulation of an ant colony called ACS

annotations.note: No ant colony optimization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Metric TSP

anchor.snippet: In the metric TSP , also known as delta-TSP or Δ-TSP, the intercity distances satisfy the triangle inequality

annotations.note: Mathlib has metric spaces but no metric-TSP problem.

annotations.label: Euclidean TSP

anchor.snippet: In the Euclidean TSP (see below), the distance between two cities is the Euclidean distance between the corresponding points

annotations.note: No Euclidean TSP formalization in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: In the rectilinear TSP, the distance between two cities is the sum of the absolute values of the differences of their x - and y -coordinates

annotations.label: Maximum metric TSP

anchor.snippet: In the maximum metric , the distance between two points is the maximum of the absolute values of differences of their x - and y -coordinates

annotations.note: No max-metric TSP formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reduction of non-metric to metric instance

anchor.snippet: a symmetric, non-metric instance can be reduced to a metric one

annotations.label: Euclidean optimal tour is a simple polygon

anchor.snippet: the optimal solution to the travelling salesman problem forms a simple polygon through all of the points
Like the general TSP, the exact Euclidean TSP is NP-hard
A discretized version of the problem with distances rounded to integers is NP-complete
With rational coordinates and the actual Euclidean metric, Euclidean TSP is known to be in the Counting Hierarchy

annotations.label: Arora–Mitchell PTAS for Euclidean TSP

anchor.snippet: there is a polynomial-time algorithm that finds a tour of length at most (1 + 1/ c ) times the optimal for geometric instances of TSP

annotations.note: No PTAS or Arora–Mitchell result in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Asymmetric TSP (definition)

anchor.snippet: The case where the distance from A to B is not equal to the distance from B to A is called asymmetric TSP

annotations.note: No asymmetric TSP definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stacker crane problem

anchor.snippet: The stacker crane problem can be viewed as a special case of the asymmetric TSP

annotations.note: No stacker crane problem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conversion of asymmetric to symmetric TSP

anchor.snippet: One option is to turn an asymmetric matrix of size N into a symmetric matrix of size 2 N

annotations.note: No such conversion construction in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Analyst's travelling salesman problem

anchor.snippet: This problem is known as the analyst's travelling salesman problem

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define the analyst's TSP.

The problem has been shown to be NP-hard

annotations.label: Svensson–Tarnawski–Végh constant-factor approximation for asymmetric TSP

anchor.snippet: In the asymmetric case with triangle inequality , in 2018, a constant factor approximation was developed by Svensson, Tarnawski, and Végh
An algorithm by Vera Traub and Jens Vygen
The best known inapproximability bound is 75/74
The corresponding maximization problem of finding the longest travelling salesman tour is approximable within 63/38
the longest tour can be approximated within 4/3 by a deterministic algorithm
